items.passage:
Hibernation is a state of greatly reduced metabolic activity that allows certain mammals to survive prolonged periods of cold and food scarcity. [■] During this dormant phase, an animal's body temperature, heart rate, and breathing fall dramatically, conserving energy reserves built up before winter. Many rodents, bats, and bears rely on this adaptation to endure months when foraging would be impossible.

The physiological changes underlying hibernation are precisely regulated rather than passive. [■] A hibernating ground squirrel, for instance, may lower its heart rate from around two hundred beats per minute to fewer than ten, while its core temperature drops close to freezing. [■] Specialized brown fat tissue generates heat during periodic arousals, and hormonal signals coordinate the timing of entry and exit. These controlled shifts protect tissues that would otherwise be damaged by such extreme conditions.

This remarkable capacity, however, carries notable costs. [■] Arousals from torpor consume a large fraction of the stored fat, and repeated cycles can weaken immune defenses. Animals emerging in spring are often depleted and vulnerable to predators. Researchers continue to investigate how hibernators repair cellular damage, since such mechanisms may suggest medical applications that remain far from practical realization.
paragraphs: Hibernation is a state of greatly reduced metabolic activity that allows certain mammals to survive prolonged periods of cold and food scarcity. [■] During this dormant phase, an animal's body temperature, heart rate, and breathing fall dramatically, conserving energy reserves built up before winter. Many rodents, bats, and bears rely on this adaptation to endure months when foraging would be impossible.

The physiological changes underlying hibernation are precisely regulated rather than passive. [■] A hibernating ground squirrel, for instance, may lower its heart rate from around two hundred beats per minute to fewer than ten, while its core temperature drops close to freezing. [■] Specialized brown fat tissue generates heat during periodic arousals, and hormonal signals coordinate the timing of entry and exit. These controlled shifts protect tissues that would otherwise be damaged by such extreme conditions.

This remarkable capacity, however, carries notable costs. [■] Arousals from torpor consume a large fraction of the stored fat, and repeated cycles can weaken immune defenses. Animals emerging in spring are often depleted and vulnerable to predators. Researchers continue to investigate how hibernators repair cellular damage, since such mechanisms may suggest medical applications that remain far from practical realization.

questions.stem: The word "dormant" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: frozen
options.B: wakeful
options.C: inactive
options.D: diseased

questions.explanation: The "dormant phase" is described as one of "greatly reduced metabolic activity" in which temperature, heart rate, and breathing fall dramatically, so "dormant" means inactive.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what happens to a hibernating ground squirrel's heart rate?

options.A: It falls from about two hundred beats per minute to under ten.
options.B: It rises sharply during the coldest winter months.
options.C: It stays near two hundred beats throughout the winter.
options.D: It stops entirely until the animal warms in spring.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states the squirrel "may lower its heart rate from around two hundred beats per minute to fewer than ten."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about the medical applications of hibernation research?

options.A: They have already been adopted in hospital treatments.
options.B: They are the main reason animals hibernate.
options.C: They reduce the immune costs that hibernators face.
options.D: They are not yet ready for real-world use.

questions.explanation: The passage says medical applications "remain far from practical realization," implying they are not yet ready for actual use.

questions.stem: Why does the author mention the ground squirrel in paragraph 2?

options.A: To argue that squirrels hibernate longer than bears do
options.B: To give a concrete example of regulated physiological change
options.C: To question whether hibernation is truly controlled
options.D: To compare squirrels with bats and rodents

questions.explanation: The squirrel appears right after the claim that changes are "precisely regulated," serving as a concrete illustration of that regulation.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, the timing of these shifts is governed by an internal biological clock rather than by external temperature alone.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after the opening definition)
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 2, after the topic sentence on regulation)
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, before the sentence on brown fat tissue)
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 3, after the topic sentence on costs)

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on precise regulation and leads naturally into the following sentence about hormonal signals coordinating "the timing of entry and exit," so it fits at slot 3.

items.passage:
East Asian calligraphy faces a persistent tension between preserving inherited forms and allowing individual expression. [■] A practitioner must master a fixed repertoire of brushstrokes, each governed by strict conventions of order, pressure, and proportion. Yet mechanical imitation of these rules produces work that critics dismiss as lifeless, lacking the personal energy that distinguishes art from mere copying.

Traditional training addresses this difficulty through prolonged imitation of revered models before any deviation is permitted. [■] A student copies the works of past masters thousands of times, internalizing their rhythms until the movements become automatic. Only after this foundation is secure may the calligrapher introduce subtle variations in spacing or speed. [■] Such disciplined repetition, paradoxically, is held to liberate rather than constrain, freeing the hand to respond spontaneously.

This approach yields expressive freedom grounded in deep technical command, yet it is not without drawbacks. [■] The years required can discourage learners, and an overemphasis on canonical models may narrow stylistic diversity. Some contemporary critics argue that excessive reverence for tradition tends to suppress the very innovation the discipline claims to cultivate, leaving the balance between control and creativity unresolved.
paragraphs: East Asian calligraphy faces a persistent tension between preserving inherited forms and allowing individual expression. [■] A practitioner must master a fixed repertoire of brushstrokes, each governed by strict conventions of order, pressure, and proportion. Yet mechanical imitation of these rules produces work that critics dismiss as lifeless, lacking the personal energy that distinguishes art from mere copying.

Traditional training addresses this difficulty through prolonged imitation of revered models before any deviation is permitted. [■] A student copies the works of past masters thousands of times, internalizing their rhythms until the movements become automatic. Only after this foundation is secure may the calligrapher introduce subtle variations in spacing or speed. [■] Such disciplined repetition, paradoxically, is held to liberate rather than constrain, freeing the hand to respond spontaneously.

This approach yields expressive freedom grounded in deep technical command, yet it is not without drawbacks. [■] The years required can discourage learners, and an overemphasis on canonical models may narrow stylistic diversity. Some contemporary critics argue that excessive reverence for tradition tends to suppress the very innovation the discipline claims to cultivate, leaving the balance between control and creativity unresolved.

questions.stem: The word "repertoire" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: audience
options.B: performance
options.C: collection
options.D: rehearsal

questions.explanation: A "fixed repertoire of brushstrokes" that a practitioner must "master" refers to a set or collection of strokes, so "repertoire" means collection.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what must a student do before introducing variations?

options.A: Invent new brushstrokes of their own design
options.B: Study the written theories of past masters
options.C: Copy the works of past masters many times
options.D: Teach the basic conventions to other learners

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states a student "copies the works of past masters thousands of times" before subtle variations are permitted.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are mentioned as features or results of traditional calligraphy training EXCEPT

options.A: internalizing the rhythms of past masters
options.B: mastering conventions of pressure and proportion
options.C: permitting subtle variations after a secure foundation
options.D: rewarding rapid innovation from the earliest stage

questions.explanation: The passage requires a "secure" foundation before any deviation and notes the long years involved, so rewarding rapid early innovation is not mentioned; the other three are stated directly.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the relationship between repetition and creativity?

options.A: Repetition permanently eliminates a calligrapher's creativity.
options.B: Repetition is thought to make later spontaneity possible.
options.C: Creativity must be exercised before any repetition begins.
options.D: Creativity and repetition are entirely unrelated skills.

questions.explanation: The passage says disciplined repetition "is held to liberate rather than constrain, freeing the hand to respond spontaneously," implying repetition enables later spontaneous expression.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'And because every stroke is permanent once the ink touches the absorbent paper, no error can be corrected after the fact.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after the sentence on tension)
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 2, after the topic sentence)
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, before the sentence on disciplined repetition)
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 3, after the topic sentence)

questions.explanation: The sentence elaborates on the strict difficulty introduced in paragraph 1 and leads into the next sentence about a "fixed repertoire" governed by "strict conventions," so it fits at slot 1.

items.passage:
A mineral is a naturally occurring solid with a definite chemical composition and an orderly internal arrangement of atoms. [■] This regular structure, known as a crystal lattice, distinguishes true minerals from glasses and other amorphous substances, whose atoms lack long-range order. Thousands of distinct minerals have been identified, though only a few dozen are common in the Earth's crust.

The properties of a mineral arise directly from its composition and atomic structure. [■] Hardness, for example, reflects the strength of the bonds holding atoms in the lattice, while cleavage describes how a crystal splits along planes of weaker bonding. [■] Color, density, and the way a surface reflects light likewise depend on these internal arrangements. Because such properties are consistent for a given mineral, geologists use them to identify specimens in the field.

These identification methods, however, have clear limits. [■] Two minerals may share an identical chemical formula yet differ in structure, a relationship called polymorphism, producing markedly different physical traits. Impurities can also alter color, making that property unreliable. For precise determination, researchers must therefore turn to laboratory techniques that may not always be readily available.
paragraphs: A mineral is a naturally occurring solid with a definite chemical composition and an orderly internal arrangement of atoms. [■] This regular structure, known as a crystal lattice, distinguishes true minerals from glasses and other amorphous substances, whose atoms lack long-range order. Thousands of distinct minerals have been identified, though only a few dozen are common in the Earth's crust.

The properties of a mineral arise directly from its composition and atomic structure. [■] Hardness, for example, reflects the strength of the bonds holding atoms in the lattice, while cleavage describes how a crystal splits along planes of weaker bonding. [■] Color, density, and the way a surface reflects light likewise depend on these internal arrangements. Because such properties are consistent for a given mineral, geologists use them to identify specimens in the field.

These identification methods, however, have clear limits. [■] Two minerals may share an identical chemical formula yet differ in structure, a relationship called polymorphism, producing markedly different physical traits. Impurities can also alter color, making that property unreliable. For precise determination, researchers must therefore turn to laboratory techniques that may not always be readily available.

questions.stem: The word "amorphous" in paragraph 1 is closest in meaning to

options.A: transparent
options.B: natural
options.C: metallic
options.D: shapeless

questions.explanation: Amorphous substances are contrasted with the "regular structure" of a crystal lattice and described as lacking "long-range order," so "amorphous" means shapeless.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what does the hardness of a mineral reflect?

options.A: The strength of the bonds holding atoms in the lattice
options.B: The presence of impurities within the crystal
options.C: The way a polished surface reflects incoming light
options.D: The number of planes along which a crystal splits

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that "hardness... reflects the strength of the bonds holding atoms in the lattice."

questions.stem: The phrase "these internal arrangements" in paragraph 2 refers to

options.A: color, density, and reflection
options.B: planes of weaker bonding
options.C: the composition and atomic structure
options.D: specimens in the field

questions.explanation: The paragraph explains that properties depend on the mineral's "composition and atomic structure," which "these internal arrangements" refers back to.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 3 about using color to identify minerals?

options.A: Color is the single most reliable diagnostic property.
options.B: Color is identical in all specimens of one mineral.
options.C: Color alone can be misleading for identification.
options.D: Color is unaffected by the presence of impurities.

questions.explanation: The passage notes that "impurities can also alter color, making that property unreliable," implying color alone can mislead.

questions.stem: There are four locations [■] in the passage that indicate where the following sentence could be added. **'For example, diamond and graphite are both pure carbon, yet one is among the hardest substances known and the other is soft enough to write with.'** Where would the sentence best fit? Select a location to add the sentence to the passage.

options.A: Slot 1 (paragraph 1, after the opening definition)
options.B: Slot 2 (paragraph 3, after the sentence on polymorphism)
options.C: Slot 3 (paragraph 2, between hardness and color)
options.D: Slot 4 (paragraph 2, after the topic sentence)

questions.explanation: The diamond-graphite example illustrates two minerals with an "identical chemical formula yet differ in structure," directly elaborating the polymorphism sentence, so it fits at slot 2.

items.passage:
The origin of human language poses a profound difficulty for researchers, since speech leaves no direct fossil trace. Unlike bones or tools, spoken words vanish the instant they are uttered, and writing appeared only within the last few thousand years. Scholars therefore cannot observe the earliest stages of language directly, and must reconstruct them from indirect evidence alone.

To overcome this obstacle, investigators draw on several converging lines of inference. The anatomy of fossil skulls reveals changes in the vocal tract and the brain regions associated with speech. Comparisons with the communication systems of other primates suggest which capacities humans may have inherited and which are unique. Archaeological signs of symbolic behavior, such as ornaments and burial practices, are taken as indirect markers of a mind capable of language.

This convergent strategy has yielded plausible hypotheses, yet firm conclusions remain elusive. Each line of evidence is open to competing interpretations, and the available material is fragmentary. Because the relevant fossils are scarce and the behavioral traces ambiguous, scholars continue to debate whether language emerged gradually or appeared relatively suddenly, and the question may never be settled with certainty.
paragraphs: The origin of human language poses a profound difficulty for researchers, since speech leaves no direct fossil trace. Unlike bones or tools, spoken words vanish the instant they are uttered, and writing appeared only within the last few thousand years. Scholars therefore cannot observe the earliest stages of language directly, and must reconstruct them from indirect evidence alone.

To overcome this obstacle, investigators draw on several converging lines of inference. The anatomy of fossil skulls reveals changes in the vocal tract and the brain regions associated with speech. Comparisons with the communication systems of other primates suggest which capacities humans may have inherited and which are unique. Archaeological signs of symbolic behavior, such as ornaments and burial practices, are taken as indirect markers of a mind capable of language.

This convergent strategy has yielded plausible hypotheses, yet firm conclusions remain elusive. Each line of evidence is open to competing interpretations, and the available material is fragmentary. Because the relevant fossils are scarce and the behavioral traces ambiguous, scholars continue to debate whether language emerged gradually or appeared relatively suddenly, and the question may never be settled with certainty.

questions.stem: The word "elusive" in paragraph 3 is closest in meaning to

options.A: expensive
options.B: unattainable
options.C: widespread
options.D: deceptive

questions.explanation: Firm conclusions are "elusive" because the evidence is fragmentary and open to competing interpretations, so the word means hard to attain or unattainable.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, what does the anatomy of fossil skulls reveal?

options.A: The exact age at which writing was first invented
options.B: The communication systems used by living primates
options.C: The symbolic meaning of ancient burial practices
options.D: Changes in the vocal tract and speech-related brain regions

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that the anatomy of fossil skulls "reveals changes in the vocal tract and the brain regions associated with speech."

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about ornaments and burial practices?

options.A: They are the oldest known form of human writing.
options.B: They directly preserve the sounds of early speech.
options.C: They are read as signs of a mind capable of language.
options.D: They prove that language emerged very suddenly.

questions.explanation: The passage treats such symbolic behavior as "indirect markers of a mind capable of language," so these practices are read as evidence of a language-capable mind.

questions.stem: Why does the author contrast spoken words with bones and tools in paragraph 1?

options.A: To emphasize why early language leaves no direct evidence
options.B: To argue that tools are more important than language
options.C: To show that bones and tools also disappear quickly
options.D: To explain how writing was eventually invented

questions.explanation: The contrast stresses that, unlike durable bones and tools, "spoken words vanish the instant they are uttered," emphasizing the lack of direct evidence.

questions.stem: According to the passage, all of the following are sources of evidence used to study language origins EXCEPT

options.A: the anatomy of fossil skulls
options.B: the communication systems of other primates
options.C: recordings of the earliest human speech
options.D: archaeological signs of symbolic behavior

questions.explanation: The passage stresses that speech leaves no direct trace and vanishes when uttered, so recordings of early speech are impossible; the other three sources are named in paragraph 2.

items.passage:
Ancient astronomy was the systematic observation of celestial bodies by early civilizations long before the invention of the telescope. Relying solely on the unaided eye, observers in Babylon, Egypt, and elsewhere tracked the movements of the Sun, Moon, and stars across the sky. Their records served practical purposes, guiding agriculture, religious festivals, and the measurement of time.

These early observations gradually produced surprisingly accurate predictive systems. Babylonian astronomers, recording planetary positions on clay tablets over centuries, detected repeating cycles that allowed them to forecast eclipses and the return of certain celestial events. Egyptian observers aligned monuments with particular stars to mark the seasons. Such achievements rested not on physical theories of why bodies moved, but on careful, long-term record-keeping of how they moved.

This observational tradition, however, had inherent limitations. Without instruments to magnify distant objects, ancient astronomers could not discern the true nature of the planets or the vast distances involved. Their models described apparent motions accurately yet often assumed an Earth-centered cosmos. Despite these constraints, the data they accumulated proved valuable to later thinkers, who would eventually reinterpret it within wholly new frameworks.
paragraphs: Ancient astronomy was the systematic observation of celestial bodies by early civilizations long before the invention of the telescope. Relying solely on the unaided eye, observers in Babylon, Egypt, and elsewhere tracked the movements of the Sun, Moon, and stars across the sky. Their records served practical purposes, guiding agriculture, religious festivals, and the measurement of time.

These early observations gradually produced surprisingly accurate predictive systems. Babylonian astronomers, recording planetary positions on clay tablets over centuries, detected repeating cycles that allowed them to forecast eclipses and the return of certain celestial events. Egyptian observers aligned monuments with particular stars to mark the seasons. Such achievements rested not on physical theories of why bodies moved, but on careful, long-term record-keeping of how they moved.

This observational tradition, however, had inherent limitations. Without instruments to magnify distant objects, ancient astronomers could not discern the true nature of the planets or the vast distances involved. Their models described apparent motions accurately yet often assumed an Earth-centered cosmos. Despite these constraints, the data they accumulated proved valuable to later thinkers, who would eventually reinterpret it within wholly new frameworks.

questions.stem: What is the passage mainly about?

options.A: How the telescope transformed the study of distant planets
options.B: Why Babylonian and Egyptian religions relied on the stars
options.C: The disputes among ancient observers over an Earth-centered cosmos
options.D: The achievements and limits of naked-eye astronomy in early civilizations

questions.explanation: The passage defines ancient astronomy, describes its predictive achievements, and then notes its limitations, matching the full scope in option D.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 2, how did Babylonian astronomers achieve their predictions?

options.A: By recording planetary positions over many centuries
options.B: By using early lenses to magnify distant planets
options.C: By developing physical theories of planetary motion
options.D: By aligning large monuments with particular stars

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says Babylonian astronomers were "recording planetary positions on clay tablets over centuries" and thereby detected repeating cycles.

questions.stem: How does paragraph 3 relate to paragraph 2?

options.A: It gives further examples that extend the achievements in paragraph 2.
options.B: It identifies limitations that qualify the achievements in paragraph 2.
options.C: It rejects the claims about predictive accuracy made in paragraph 2.
options.D: It restates the practical purposes introduced in paragraph 2.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 describes accurate predictive achievements, and paragraph 3 turns with "however" to the "inherent limitations" of that tradition, qualifying it.

questions.stem: What can be inferred from paragraph 2 about the basis of ancient predictive systems?

options.A: Accurate prediction did not require understanding why bodies move.
options.B: Prediction was impossible without a correct theory of motion.
options.C: Egyptian observers contributed more than Babylonian ones did.
options.D: The clay-tablet records were largely inaccurate over time.

questions.explanation: The passage states achievements rested "not on physical theories of why bodies moved, but on careful, long-term record-keeping," implying prediction did not require knowing why bodies move.

questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what did the models of ancient astronomers often assume?

options.A: That the planets were far larger than the Earth
options.B: That the unaided eye could resolve distant objects
options.C: That the cosmos was centered on the Earth
options.D: That eclipses could not be predicted in advance

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 states their models "often assumed an Earth-centered cosmos."
